title: 2026 FIFA World Cup squads - Wikipedia
original_text:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup is an ongoing international football tournament being held in Canada, Mexico, and the United States, from June 11 to July 19, 2026. The 48 national teams involved in the tournament were required to register a squad of up to 26 players, including three goalkeepers. Only players in these squads will be eligible to take part in the tournament.[1]
A provisional release list of between 35 and 55 players per national team had to be submitted to FIFA by May 11, 2026, one month prior to the opening match of the tournament. The release lists were not made public by FIFA. From the preliminary squad, the final list of at least 23 and at most 26 players (at least three of whom shall be goalkeepers) per national team had to be submitted to FIFA by June 1, ten days prior to the opening match of the tournament.[2] The squad lists were published by FIFA the following day.[3] In the event that a player on the submitted squad list suffers from an injury or illness prior to his team's first match of the tournament, that player could be replaced at any time up to 24 hours before their first match. The team doctor and the FIFA General Medical Officer have to both confirm that the injury or illness is severe enough to prevent the player from participating in the tournament. Replacement players are limited to the preliminary list.[1]
The position listed for each player is per the official squad list published by FIFA. The age listed for each player is as of June 11, 2026, the first day of the tournament. The numbers of caps and goals listed for each player do not include any matches played after the start of the tournament. The club listed is the club for which the player last played a competitive match prior to the tournament.[note 1] The nationality for each club reflects the national association (not the league) to which the club is affiliated. A flag is included for coaches who are of a different nationality to their team.
Group A
Czech Republic
Coach: Miroslav Koubek
The Czech Republic announced a 54-man preliminary squad on May 12.[4] The squad was reduced to 29 players on May 21.[5] They announced their final squad on May 31.[6]
Mexico
Coach: Javier Aguirre
Mexico announced a 55-man preliminary squad on May 12.[7] The squad was reduced to 51 players on May 23.[8] They announced their final squad on May 31.[9]
South Africa
Coach: Hugo Broos
South Africa announced a 32-man preliminary squad on May 21.[10] They announced their final squad on May 27.[11][12]
South Korea
Coach: Hong Myung-bo
South Korea announced their final squad on May 16.[13] Cho Yu-min withdrew injured and was replaced by Cho Wi-je on May 31.[14]
Group B
Bosnia and Herzegovina
Coach: Sergej Barbarez
Bosnia and Herzegovina announced their final squad on May 11.[15][16] Osman Hadžikić withdrew injured and was replaced by Mladen Jurkas on June 1.[17] Nidal Čelik withdrew injured and was replaced by Arjan Malić on June 11.[18]
Canada
Coach: Jesse Marsch
Canada announced a 32-man preliminary squad on May 25.[19] They announced their final squad on May 29.[20] Marcelo Flores withdrew injured on May 31 and was replaced by Jayden Nelson on June 9.[21][22]
Qatar
Coach: Julen Lopetegui
Qatar announced a 34-man preliminary squad on May 12.[23] The squad was extended to 35 players on May 18,[24] then reduced to 28 players on May 25.[25] They announced their final squad on June 1.[26]
Switzerland
Coach: Murat Yakin
Switzerland announced their final squad through a social media campaign on May 18 and 19,[27] which was officially confirmed on May 20.[28]
Group C
Brazil
Coach: Carlo Ancelotti
Brazil announced a 55-man preliminary squad on May 12.[29][30] They announced their final squad on May 18.[31] Wesley withdrew injured and was replaced by Éderson Silva on June 7.[32]
Haiti
Coach: Sébastien Migné
Haiti announced their final squad on May 15.[33][34] Leverton Pierre withdrew injured and was replaced by Garven Metusala on June 11.[35]
Morocco
Coach: Mohamed Ouahbi
Morocco announced their final squad on May 26.[36] Abde Ezzalzouli and Nayef Aguerd withdrew injured and were replaced by Amine Sbaï and Marwane Saâdane on June 10, respectively.[37]
Scotland
Coach: Steve Clarke
Scotland announced their final squad on May 19.[38] Billy Gilmour withdrew injured on May 30,[39] and was replaced by Tyler Fletcher on May 31.[40]
Group D
Australia
Coach: Tony Popovic
Australia announced their final squad on May 31.[41]
Paraguay
Coach: Gustavo Alfaro
Paraguay announced a 55-man preliminary squad on May 12.[42] They announced their final squad on June 1.[43][44]
Turkey
Coach: Vincenzo Montella
Turkey announced a 35-man preliminary squad on May 18.[45] Their final squad was announced on June 2.[46]
United States
Coach: Mauricio Pochettino
The United States announced their final squad on May 26.[47]
Group E
Curaçao
Coach: Dick Advocaat
Curaçao announced their final squad on May 18.[48][49]
Ecuador
Coach: Sebastián Beccacece
Ecuador announced their final squad on May 31.[50][51]
Germany
Coach: Julian Nagelsmann
Germany announced their final squad on May 21.[52] Lennart Karl withdrew injured and was replaced by Assan Ouédraogo on June 5.[53]
Ivory Coast
Coach: Emerse Faé
Ivory Coast announced their final squad on May 15.[54][55] Clément Akpa withdrew injured and was replaced by Christopher Opéri on May 29.[56]
Group F
Japan
Coach: Hajime Moriyasu
Japan announced their final squad on May 15.[57] Wataru Endo withdrew injured and was replaced by Shūto Machino on June 11.[58]
Netherlands
Coach: Ronald Koeman
The Netherlands announced their final squad on May 27.[59] Jurriën Timber withdrew injured and was replaced by Lutsharel Geertruida on June 8.[60]
Sweden
Coach: Graham Potter
Sweden announced their final squad on May 12.[61] Emil Holm withdrew injured and was replaced by Herman Johansson on May 30.[62]
Tunisia
Coach: Sabri Lamouchi
Tunisia announced their final squad on May 15.[63][64]
Group G
Belgium
Coach: Rudi Garcia
Belgium announced their final squad on May 15.[65]
Egypt
Coach: Hossam Hassan
Egypt announced a 27-man preliminary squad on May 20.[66] They announced their final squad on May 30.[67]
Iran
Coach: Amir Ghalenoei
Iran announced a 30-man preliminary squad on May 16.[68] The squad was extended to 31 players on May 21.[69] They announced their final squad on June 1.[70][71]
New Zealand
Coach: Darren Bazeley
New Zealand announced their final squad on May 14.[72]
Group H
Cape Verde
Coach: Bubista
Cape Verde announced their final squad on May 18.[73]
Saudi Arabia
Coach: Georgios Donis
Saudi Arabia announced a 30-man preliminary squad on May 23.[74] They announced their final squad on May 31.[75][76]
Spain
Coach: Luis de la Fuente
Spain announced their final squad on May 25.[77]
Uruguay
Coach: Marcelo Bielsa
Uruguay announced their final squad on May 31.[78][79]
Group I
France
Coach: Didier Deschamps
France announced their final squad on May 14.[80]
Iraq
Coach: Graham Arnold
Iraq announced a 34-man preliminary squad on May 19.[81][82] They announced their final squad on June 1.[83] Ahmed Yahya withdrew injured with ​a hamstring injury and was replaced by Ahmed Maknzi on June 6.[84]
Norway
Coach: Ståle Solbakken
Norway announced their final squad on May 21.[85]
Senegal
Coach: Pape Thiaw
Senegal announced a 28-man preliminary squad on May 21.[86] They announced their final squad on June 1.[87]
Group J
Algeria
Coach: Vladimir Petković
Algeria announced their final squad on May 31.[88][89]
Argentina
Coach: Lionel Scaloni
Argentina announced a 55-man preliminary squad on May 11.[90] They announced their final squad on May 28.[91] Leonardo Balerdi withdrew due to a calf injury on June 6[92] and was replaced by Marcos Senesi on June 11.[93]
Austria
Coach: Ralf Rangnick
Austria announced their final squad on May 18.[94] Christoph Baumgartner withdrew injured on June 2[95] and was replaced by Dejan Ljubičić on June 10.[96]
Jordan
Coach: Jamal Sellami
Jordan announced a 30-man preliminary squad on May 17.[97] The squad was reduced to 28 players on May 27.[98] Their final squad was announced on June 2.[99] Ibrahim Sabra withdrew injured on June 5[100] and was replaced by Mohammad Taha on June 8.[101]
Group K
Colombia
Coach: Néstor Lorenzo
Colombia announced a 55-man preliminary squad on May 14.[102] They announced their final squad on May 25.[103]
DR Congo
Coach: Sébastien Desabre
DR Congo announced their final squad on May 18.[104][105] Rocky Bushiri withdrew injured and was replaced by Aaron Tshibola on May 20.[106][107]
Portugal
Coach: Roberto Martínez
Portugal announced their final squad on May 19.[108][109]
Uzbekistan
Coach: Fabio Cannavaro
Uzbekistan announced a 40-man preliminary squad on May 5.[110] The squad was reduced to 39 players on May 22 as Ibrokhimkhalil Yuldoshev withdrew injured,[111] then to 30 players on May 24.[112] Their final squad was announced on June 2.[113]
Group L
Croatia
Coach: Zlatko Dalić
Croatia announced their final squad on May 18.[114]
England
Coach: Thomas Tuchel
England announced their final squad on May 22.[115]
Ghana
Coach: Carlos Queiroz
Ghana announced a 28-man preliminary squad on May 25.[116] Alexander Djiku withdrew injured and was replaced by Derrick Luckassen on June 1.[117] They announced their final squad on June 2.[118]
Panama
Coach: Thomas Christiansen
Panama announced their final squad on May 26.[119]

None of the squads are made up entirely of players from the countries' domestic leagues. The Qatar and Saudi Arabia squads have 25 players from their respective leagues.[120]
The Cape Verde, Curaçao, DR Congo, Ivory Coast, Senegal and Uruguay squads don't include any players employed by clubs in their home country.[120]
Uruguay league is the only domestic league represented at the tournament which doesn't provide any player for their national team.
The Canada and New Zealand squads both include players employed by domestic clubs, but no players employed in the Canadian or New Zealand domestic league systems. All Canadian domestic players play for Canadian teams in the American Major League Soccer; all New Zealand domestic players play for New Zealand teams in the Australian A-League Men.
The Netherlands squad has the most players from a single foreign federation, with 15 players employed in England.
Of the countries not represented by a national team at the tournament, Italy's league provided the most squad members, with 71.[120]
The lowest leagues on a domestic pyramid to be represented at the tournament are National League, the English fifth tier, and Oberliga, the German fifth tier. They are represented by New Zealand player Tommy Smith (Braintree Town) and Haiti player Josué Duverger (Cosmos Koblenz), respectively.

Notes
^ This is the club a player was last able to play for during the previous season in the event a player did not play a competitive match.
^ Includes players from Swansea City and Wrexham, which are based in Wales but play in the English league system.
^ Includes five players from the third tier and one player from the fifth tier.
^ Includes one player from the fifth tier.
^ Includes players from Monaco, which is based in Monaco but plays in the French league system. Monaco is not a FIFA member.
^ Includes one player from the third tier.
^ Includes players from Toronto FC and Vancouver Whitecaps FC, which are based in Canada but play in the American league system.
^ Includes players from Auckland FC and Wellington Phoenix, which are based in New Zealand but play in the Australian league system.
